item.title: What will you regret? Studying sabbaticals taught me a hard truth.
item.description: My research revealed we rue inaction far more than the things we actually do.

item.title: How to make ICE smart again
item.description: Enforcing immigration law doesn’t preclude reforming it. Traffic stops have got to go.

item.title: Hosting a prayer at your house? You might have to ‘cease and desist.’
item.description: Daniel Grand invited friends to a group prayer at his home. Local officials “put a stop to it.”

item.title: The electric fence lessons of an ’80s summer
item.description: Escaping suburban bubbles meant trading cable TV for farm life.

item.title: What America got wrong about drug addiction
item.description:
The drug crisis in the United States did not begin with fentanyl. It began with a series of choices that seemed compassionate at the time — prescribing opioids for people in pain, scaling back punishment and trying to make drug use safer. But addiction spread, fentanyl changed the stakes, and the solutions no longer fit the crises unfolding in communities.
Host Megan McArdle talks with Keith Humphreys, a Stanford professor and psychologist, about what America got wrong about drug addiction, why overdose deaths are finally falling, where harm reduction lost its way and what a better approach could look like.

item.title: States stand in the way of a nuclear energy renaissance
item.description: The federal government is only one layer of the regulatory cake.

item.title: Trump’s support for Lebanon is worth cheering
item.description: Joseph Aoun, the country’s president, left D.C. with the backing he needs to stem decades of chaos.

item.title: Why have political double standards gotten worse?
item.description: Are double standards becoming the norm in American politics? In this episode of “Make It Make Sense,” Carine Hajjar and Brad Palumbo, an independent journalist and YouTube creator, break down the growing fractures on both the left and the right. They discuss recent Senate races involving Graham Platner and Abdul El-Sayed; the controversy surrounding Ken Paxton in Texas; and how media echo chambers, viral clips and conspiracy theories are pushing the country further apart.

item.title: Should kratom be criminalized? Follow the science.
item.description: Kratom, social media use in teens, New York's data center moratorium and more, in readers' eyes.

item.title: Surprise, surprise. Trump’s Qatar Force One already has issues.
item.description: The president should have rejected the Qatari plane. Now he has a perfect excuse to send it back.

item.title: This failed Tennessee dam should stay dead
item.description: The costs of damming the Duck River — fiscal and environmental — are too high.

item.title: The AI tax movement is built on a myth
item.description: Washington wants to help American workers. Taxing artificial intelligence would do the opposite.

item.title: Darline Graham’s appointment belongs to a pattern — with one difference
item.description: A new twist in the tradition of appointing family members of deceased senators to fill vacancies.

item.title: Tylenol lawsuits are asking the wrong question
item.description: Science still points to acetaminophen as the safest choice for pain and fever during pregnancy.

item.title: Department of War games
item.description: Michael Ramirez cartoon on Pete Hegseth, military politicization.

item.title: Should daylight saving time really be permanent?
item.description: Daylight saving time, Boomer capital, degree cost and more, in readers' eyes.
